(**NY 63**) is a [state highway](/wiki/State_highway "State highway") in the western part of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") in the United States. It extends for 82.11 miles (132.14 km) in a generally southeast–northwest direction from an intersection with [NY 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15") and [NY 21](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_21 "New York State Route 21") in the [village of Wayland](/wiki/Wayland_(village),_New_York "Wayland (village), New York") in [Steuben County](/wiki/Steuben_County,_New_York "Steuben County, New York") to a junction with [NY 18](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_18 "New York State Route 18") in the town of [Yates](/wiki/Yates,_New_York "Yates, New York") in [Orleans County](/wiki/Orleans_County,_New_York "Orleans County, New York"), 2 miles (3.2 km) south of the [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario") shoreline. The route passes through the [city of Batavia](/wiki/Batavia,_New_York "Batavia, New York") and enters or comes near several villages, including [Dansville](/wiki/Dansville,_Livingston_County,_New_York "Dansville, Livingston County, New York") and [Medina](/wiki/Medina,_New_York "Medina, New York").

**New York State Route 63** (**NY 63**) is a [state highway](/wiki/State_highway "State highway") in the western part of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") in the United States. It extends for 82.11 miles (132.14 km) in a generally southeast–northwest direction from an intersection with [NY 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15") and [NY 21](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_21 "New York State Route 21") in the [village of Wayland](/wiki/Wayland_(village),_New_York "Wayland (village), New York") in [Steuben County](/wiki/Steuben_County,_New_York "Steuben County, New York") to a junction with [NY 18](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_18 "New York State Route 18") in the town of [Yates](/wiki/Yates,_New_York "Yates, New York") in [Orleans County](/wiki/Orleans_County,_New_York "Orleans County, New York"), 2 miles (3.2 km) south of the [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario") shoreline. The route passes through the [city of Batavia](/wiki/Batavia,_New_York "Batavia, New York") and enters or comes near several villages, including [Dansville](/wiki/Dansville,_Livingston_County,_New_York "Dansville, Livingston County, New York") and [Medina](/wiki/Medina,_New_York "Medina, New York").

NY 63 was assigned as part of the [1930 renumbering of state highways in New York](/wiki/1930_state_highway_renumbering_(New_York) "1930 state highway renumbering (New York)"), but to a largely different routing than it follows today. The original alignment of NY 63 was identical to its current alignment between [Mount Morris](/wiki/Mount_Morris,_New_York "Mount Morris, New York") and [Pavilion](/wiki/Pavilion,_New_York "Pavilion, New York"); however, the route deviated significantly from its modern routing past those points as it extended southwest from Mount Morris to [Hinsdale](/wiki/Hinsdale,_New_York "Hinsdale, New York") and north from Pavilion to [Hamlin](/wiki/Hamlin,_New_York "Hamlin, New York"). It was rerouted north of Pavilion c. 1939 and south of Mount Morris in the early 1940s. The latter realignment supplanted **New York State Route 36A**, a Dansville–Mount Morris highway assigned in 1930. For a brief period during the 1970s, NY 63 began in Dansville instead of Wayland.

## Route description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_63&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Route description")]

### Wayland to Geneseo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_63&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Wayland to Geneseo")]

NY 63 begins at a four-way intersection with [NY 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15") and [NY 21](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_21 "New York State Route 21") in the [village of Wayland](/wiki/Wayland_(village),_New_York "Wayland (village), New York"), located in northern [Steuben County](/wiki/Steuben_County,_New_York "Steuben County, New York"). It heads west through the village on the two-lane West Naples Street to a less developed part of the [town of Wayland](/wiki/Wayland,_New_York "Wayland, New York"), where it parallels [Interstate 390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") (I-390) on the north side of a wide valley. The route soon enters [Livingston County](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York") and the town of [North Dansville](/wiki/North_Dansville,_New_York "North Dansville, New York"), gaining the name Main Street as it bends northwestward into the village of [Dansville](/wiki/Dansville,_Livingston_County,_New_York "Dansville, Livingston County, New York"). Here, it [overlaps](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") with [NY 36](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_36 "New York State Route 36") for one block through the village center, beginning at Clara Barton Street and ending at Ossian Street. After another block, NY 63 meets the south end of [NY 256](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_256 "New York State Route 256") at Perine Street. While NY 256 heads north toward [Conesus Lake](/wiki/Conesus_Lake "Conesus Lake"), NY 63 proceeds northwest past [Dansville Municipal Airport](/wiki/Dansville_Municipal_Airport "Dansville Municipal Airport") and out of the village limits.[[3]](#cite_note-google-3)

[![A paved two-lane road curving to the left into the distance through a large grassy area below a blue sky filled with cumulus clouds](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/NY_63_N_of_Geneseo.jpg/250px-NY_63_N_of_Geneseo.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY_63_N_of_Geneseo.jpg)

Flats along NY 63 north of Geneseo

Continuing north into the town of [Sparta](/wiki/Sparta,_New_York "Sparta, New York"), NY 63 runs along the east side of a wide, rural valley surrounding [Canaseraga Creek](/wiki/Canaseraga_Creek "Canaseraga Creek"), with NY 36 and I-390 following the west side. The route eventually reaches the town of [Groveland](/wiki/Groveland,_New_York "Groveland, New York") and [hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(New_York) "Hamlet (New York)") of Groveland Station, where [NY 258](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_258 "New York State Route 258"), a connector to NY 36, comes in from the west at the town line. Another long, open stretch brings the route to the vicinity of the [village of Mount Morris](/wiki/Mount_Morris_(village),_New_York "Mount Morris (village), New York"), where it runs much closer to I-390 and indirectly connects to the expressway by way of [NY 408](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_408 "New York State Route 408") at Hampton Corners. From this point north, the road becomes busier as NY 63 is the primary route to the [village of Geneseo](/wiki/Geneseo_(village),_New_York "Geneseo (village), New York") for northbound traffic on I-390 itself, as there is no exit at the point where the expressway crosses under NY 63. I-390 ultimately bypasses Geneseo to the southeast while NY 63 heads north toward the village, briefly joining with [U.S. Route 20A](/wiki/U.S._Route_20A_(New_York) "U.S. Route 20A (New York)") (US 20A) and [NY 39](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_39 "New York State Route 39") just outside the village limits.[[3]](#cite_note-google-3)

### Geneseo to Batavia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_63&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geneseo to Batavia")]

The highway enters Geneseo from the south, taking the name Genesee Street as it passes the western edge of the campus of [SUNY Geneseo](/wiki/State_University_of_New_York_at_Geneseo "State University of New York at Geneseo"). At the edge of the campus, the route crosses the [Genesee River](/wiki/Genesee_River "Genesee River") and exits Geneseo. Past the river in the town of [York](/wiki/York,_New_York "York, New York"), NY 63 begins to curve northwest up and out of the Genesee River valley until it runs east–west once again at the hamlet of Piffard. The major junction in York is the community of Greigsville, where NY 63 reconnects with NY 36. This next section of highway has become a major shortcut for traffic heading to the [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") area, despite remaining a two-lane road through open rural country, since it is both physically shorter than going all the way to the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") as well as [toll](/wiki/Toll_road "Toll road")-free. Most of this Buffalo-bound traffic follows NY 36 north from Mount Morris and turns on to NY 63 here. Signage along this route reflects this use.[[3]](#cite_note-google-3)

[![Two green signs on metal poles with white lettering and arrows pointing to the left. The larger one says "Buffalo area" and the smaller says "Trucks to Warsaw". There is a road behind it to the left leading to an intersection at the rear.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Buffalo_sign_at_NYS_36-63_junction.jpg/250px-Buffalo_sign_at_NYS_36-63_junction.jpg)](/wiki/File:Buffalo_sign_at_NYS_36-63_junction.jpg)

Sign at Greigsville directing Buffalo-bound traffic onto NY 63 from NY 36

From Greigsville, the route heads west through open land into the northeast corner of [Wyoming County](/wiki/Wyoming_County,_New_York "Wyoming County, New York") and the town of [Covington](/wiki/Covington,_New_York "Covington, New York"). At Peoria, the highway turns to head due northwest, its direction for the next 30 miles (48 km).[[3]](#cite_note-google-3) The bend at Peoria was once a sharp, accident-prone turn known as Peoria Curve;[[4]](#cite_note-DOT63-4) however, the route has been slightly realigned to the north to create a longer, more gradual curve. From Peoria, NY 63 runs across rolling, open terrain to the [Genesee County](/wiki/Genesee_County,_New_York "Genesee County, New York") line and the town of [Pavilion](/wiki/Pavilion,_New_York "Pavilion, New York"). Just past the county line, the route connects to the northern end of [NY 246](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_246 "New York State Route 246"). A mile (1.6 km) beyond, NY 63 drops down slightly to intersect with [NY 19](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_19 "New York State Route 19") at the hamlet of Pavilion. After the [traffic light](/wiki/Traffic_light "Traffic light") at the center of the hamlet, NY 63 crosses [Oatka Creek](/wiki/Oatka_Creek "Oatka Creek") and climbs back up out of the Wyoming Valley.[[3]](#cite_note-google-3)

Once atop the hill, it continues due northwest to its next junction, the underdeveloped crossing of [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York"). Here at least some Buffalo-bound traffic will turn west. Eventually acquiring the name Ellicott Street, NY 63 crosses sparsely populated parts of the towns of [Bethany](/wiki/Bethany,_New_York "Bethany, New York") and [Batavia](/wiki/Batavia_(town),_New_York "Batavia (town), New York") on its way to the [city of Batavia](/wiki/Batavia,_New_York "Batavia, New York"). The route passes under the [Depew, Lancaster and Western Railroad](/wiki/Depew,_Lancaster_and_Western_Railroad "Depew, Lancaster and Western Railroad") and the [CSX Transportation](/wiki/CSX_Transportation "CSX Transportation")-owned [Rochester Subdivision](/wiki/Rochester_Subdivision "Rochester Subdivision") rail line on its way into downtown, where it intersects the two major east–west trunk routes in this corridor, [NY 5](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5 "New York State Route 5") and [NY 33](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_33 "New York State Route 33"). It overlaps with both roads for several blocks along Main Street, with NY 33 splitting off at Oak Street. This junction is also where NY 63 meets the north–south [NY 98](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_98 "New York State Route 98"), which connects to the Thruway just north of the city.[[3]](#cite_note-google-3)

### Batavia to Yates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_63&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Batavia to Yates")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/NY_63-31_in_Medina.jpg/250px-NY_63-31_in_Medina.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY_63-31_in_Medina.jpg)

NY 63 and NY 31 north through Medina village

NY 63 forks from NY 5 at the western city line, returning to the town of Batavia, changing names to Lewiston Road as it passes by [Batavia Downs](/wiki/Batavia_Downs "Batavia Downs") and runs northwest from downtown. The highway crosses over the Thruway with no access to the highway on its way across another rural stretch leading to the [village of Oakfield](/wiki/Oakfield_(village),_New_York "Oakfield (village), New York"), located in the town of the same name, where [NY 262](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_262 "New York State Route 262") departs to the east. Just north of Oakfield, NY 63 turns due west on Judge Road, with Lewiston Road continuing northwest as [County Route 12](/wiki/County_Route_12_(Genesee_County,_New_York) "County Route 12 (Genesee County, New York)") (CR 12). NY 63 follows Judge Road into the town of [Alabama](/wiki/Alabama,_New_York "Alabama, New York"), passing through the hamlet of South Alabama on its way to an undeveloped junction with [NY 77](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_77 "New York State Route 77") north of Basom. NY 63 turns north here, overlapping with NY 77 for 1.5 miles (2.4 km) to the hamlet of Alabama. In the center of the community, NY 63 reconnects to CR 12, and NY 77 turns west to follow the county road to the [Niagara County](/wiki/Niagara_County,_New_York "Niagara County, New York") line.[[3]](#cite_note-google-3)

Past Alabama, NY 63 continues northward across the [Iroquois National Wildlife Refuge](/wiki/Iroquois_National_Wildlife_Refuge "Iroquois National Wildlife Refuge") and into [Orleans County](/wiki/Orleans_County,_New_York "Orleans County, New York"). It traverses open, undeveloped areas of the town of [Shelby](/wiki/Shelby,_New_York "Shelby, New York") to reach the village of [Medina](/wiki/Medina,_New_York "Medina, New York"). This village begins at a junction with [NY 31](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_31 "New York State Route 31") and [NY 31A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_31A "New York State Route 31A"). The latter highway continues eastward while the former joins NY 63 through the [village's historic central district](/wiki/Main_Street_Historic_District_(Medina,_New_York) "Main Street Historic District (Medina, New York)") on Main Street. At Center Street, [NY 31E](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_31E "New York State Route 31E") comes in from the west and NY 31 leaves NY 63 to continue east. NY 63 continues northwest on Main and Commercial streets to the edge of the village, where it turns northward onto Prospect Avenue and subsequently crosses over the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal").[[3]](#cite_note-google-3) The northernmost two blocks of the overlap with NY 31 are maintained by the village of Medina,[[5]](#cite_note-medina_quad-5) as are the two blocks of Main Street leading away from the north end of the concurrency. This is the only segment of NY 63 not maintained by the [New York State Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation") (NYSDOT).[[6]](#cite_note-nys-hwylaw-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Downtown_Medina%2C_NY.jpg/250px-Downtown_Medina%2C_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:Downtown_Medina,_NY.jpg)

NY 63 northbound in Medina

For the next 3 miles (4.8 km), the highway serves a stretch of scattered homes along the western bank of [Oak Orchard Creek](/wiki/Oak_Orchard_Creek "Oak Orchard Creek"). It traverses a mix of fields and forests to reach the town and hamlet of [Ridgeway](/wiki/Ridgeway,_New_York "Ridgeway, New York"), the latter located at NY 63's junction with [NY 104](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_104 "New York State Route 104"). NY 63 briefly overlaps the east–west trunk road before resuming a northerly, downhill alignment toward the town of [Yates](/wiki/Yates,_New_York "Yates, New York"). The route passes through the village of [Lyndonville](/wiki/Lyndonville,_New_York "Lyndonville, New York") as Main Street, crossing over Johnson Creek in the center of the community before intersecting [NY 18](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_18 "New York State Route 18") about 1.5 miles (2.4 km) north of the village limits in the hamlet of Yates Center. NY 63 ends here while the highway continues north toward [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario") as Lyndonville Road.[[3]](#cite_note-google-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_63&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins and designation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_63&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Origins and designation")]

The [New York State Legislature](/wiki/New_York_State_Legislature "New York State Legislature") created a statewide system of unsigned [legislative routes](/wiki/Legislative_route "Legislative route") in 1908, with two of the routes using parts of what is now NY 63. The stretch of NY 63 connecting [Dansville](/wiki/Dansville,_Livingston_County,_New_York "Dansville, Livingston County, New York") to Hampton Corners in the [town of Mount Morris](/wiki/Mount_Morris,_New_York "Mount Morris, New York") became part of Route 15, a highway continuing south to [Hornell](/wiki/Hornell,_New_York "Hornell, New York") and north through [Mount Morris](/wiki/Mount_Morris_(village),_New_York "Mount Morris (village), New York") to [Caledonia](/wiki/Caledonia_(village),_New_York "Caledonia (village), New York"). Farther north, the piece between [Medina](/wiki/Medina,_New_York "Medina, New York") and [Ridgeway](/wiki/Ridgeway,_New_York "Ridgeway, New York") was designated as part of Route 30, a cross-state route running from [Niagara Falls](/wiki/Niagara_Falls,_New_York "Niagara Falls, New York") to [Rouses Point](/wiki/Rouses_Point,_New_York "Rouses Point, New York"). Route 30 originally followed current [NY 31](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_31 "New York State Route 31") to [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York");[[7]](#cite_note-1909book-7)[[8]](#cite_note-1920book-8) however, it was realigned on March 1, 1921, to use [Ridge Road](/wiki/Ridge_Road_(Western_New_York) "Ridge Road (Western New York)") instead, bypassing the Medina–Ridgeway highway.[[9]](#cite_note-1921book-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/NY_63_north_in_Lyndonville.jpg/250px-NY_63_north_in_Lyndonville.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY_63_north_in_Lyndonville.jpg)

NY 63 northbound at Johnson Creek in Lyndonville

In the mid-1920s, three sections of modern NY 63 received posted route numbers for the first time. From Wayland to Dansville, the road was the westernmost part of [NY 52](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_52_(1920s%E2%80%931930) "New York State Route 52 (1920s–1930)"). What is now NY 63 was unnumbered from Dansville northwest to Hampton Corners, where [NY 36](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_36 "New York State Route 36") entered from the west on current [NY 408](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_408 "New York State Route 408") and followed the path of NY 63 to [Geneseo](/wiki/Geneseo_(village),_New_York "Geneseo (village), New York"). The road was unnumbered again until [Pavilion](/wiki/Pavilion,_New_York "Pavilion, New York"), at which point [NY 62](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_62_(1920s%E2%80%931930) "New York State Route 62 (1920s–1930)") joined from the south and utilized all of current NY 63 and CR 63-1 to reach the Lake Ontario shoreline in Yates.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-1926map-11) By 1926, all numbered portions of current NY 63 were state-maintained, as were the unnumbered parts from Geneseo to Piffard and from [Groveland](/wiki/Groveland,_New_York "Groveland, New York") to Hampton Corners.[[11]](#cite_note-1926map-11)

NY 63 was assigned as part of the [1930 renumbering of state highways in New York](/wiki/1930_state_highway_renumbering_(New_York) "1930 state highway renumbering (New York)"). The initial routing of NY 63 was significantly different from its modern alignment; in fact, the only portion of modern NY 63 that still follows its original alignment is the section between Hampton Corners and [Pavilion](/wiki/Pavilion,_New_York "Pavilion, New York"). South of Mount Morris, the route followed what is now [NY 408](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_408 "New York State Route 408") to Dalton[[2]](#cite_note-1930nyt-2) and continued south on Old State Road and west on Allegany County's [CR 16](/wiki/County_Route_16_(Allegany_County,_New_York) "County Route 16 (Allegany County, New York)") to [NY 19](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_19 "New York State Route 19") west of the village of [Angelica](/wiki/Angelica_(village),_New_York "Angelica (village), New York"). NY 63 [overlapped](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") NY 19 south to Belvidere,[[12]](#cite_note-1930map-12) where NY 63 turned onto modern [CR 20](/wiki/County_Route_20_(Allegany_County,_New_York) "County Route 20 (Allegany County, New York)"). It continued west on CR 20 and [NY 446](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_446 "New York State Route 446") through [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba_(village),_New_York "Cuba (village), New York") to a junction with [NY 16](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_16 "New York State Route 16") in [Hinsdale](/wiki/Hinsdale,_New_York "Hinsdale, New York"), where it ended. North of Pavilion, NY 63 followed modern [NY 19](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_19 "New York State Route 19") to the [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario") shoreline in [Hamlin](/wiki/Hamlin,_New_York "Hamlin, New York").[[2]](#cite_note-1930nyt-2)

### Alignment changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_63&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Alignment changes")]

The portion of what is now NY 63 north of Pavilion was designated as part of NY 19 in the 1930 renumbering. However, unlike current NY 63, NY 19 continued north for another 2 miles (3.2 km) on Lyndonville Road past [NY 18](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_18 "New York State Route 18") to the Lake Ontario shoreline.[[2]](#cite_note-1930nyt-2) South of Mount Morris, modern NY 63 was designated as NY 36A from Mount Morris to [Dansville](/wiki/Dansville,_Livingston_County,_New_York "Dansville, Livingston County, New York"). From Dansville to [Wayland](/wiki/Wayland_(village),_New_York "Wayland (village), New York"), the highway was part of [NY 39](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_39 "New York State Route 39") (later [NY 245](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_245 "New York State Route 245")), a new route that replaced NY 52 in the 1930 renumbering.[[2]](#cite_note-1930nyt-2)[[12]](#cite_note-1930map-12) The first change to NY 63 came c. 1939 when the alignments of NY 19 and NY 63 were swapped, placing both routes on their modern alignments.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) NY 63 was altered again in December 1940 to follow the former routing of NY 36A south from Mount Morris to Dansville, from where it continued east to Wayland by way of an overlap with NY 245.[[15]](#cite_note-ny36acanned-15)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/NY_63_north_in_Oakfield.jpg/250px-NY_63_north_in_Oakfield.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY_63_north_in_Oakfield.jpg)

NY 63 north near NY 262 in Oakfield

NY 245 was truncated northeastward to [Naples](/wiki/Naples_(village),_New_York "Naples (village), New York") c. 1972[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) and NY 63 was cut back to NY 36 in Dansville around the same time,[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) leaving the Dansville–Wayland highway as an unsigned [reference route](/wiki/Reference_route_(New_York) "Reference route (New York)").[[20]](#cite_note-wayland_quad-20) This was partially reversed in the late 1970s or early 1980s when NY 63 was reextended to Wayland.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) On April 1, 1989, ownership and maintenance of Lyndonville Road north of NY 18 was transferred from the state of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") to [Orleans County](/wiki/Orleans_County,_New_York "Orleans County, New York") as part of a highway maintenance swap between the two levels of government.[[6]](#cite_note-nys-hwylaw-6) NY 63 was truncated to end at its junction with NY 18 while its former routing to the lake became CR 63-1.[[23]](#cite_note-lyndonville_quad-23)[[24]](#cite_note-inv-orleans-24)

In the mid-2000s, the route was reconfigured in the northern part of Medina to use Main and Commercial streets instead of Center Street and Prospect Avenue. The change was made as part of a village project known as the Pass Thru Project,[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) and the realignment eliminated a three-block overlap with the easternmost part of [NY 31E](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_31E "New York State Route 31E") on Center Street.[[5]](#cite_note-medina_quad-5) Ownership and maintenance of NY 63's former alignment was transferred from the state to the village on July 1, 2010, as part of a highway maintenance swap that gave Commercial Street and the northernmost block of Main Street to the state.[[6]](#cite_note-nys-hwylaw-6)

### Proposed Mount Morris–Pavilion bypass

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_63&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Proposed Mount Morris–Pavilion bypass")]

As part of a large scale study in the early 2000s, [NYSDOT](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation") determined that NY 63 from [Mount Morris](/wiki/Mount_Morris_(village),_New_York "Mount Morris (village), New York") to [Pavilion](/wiki/Pavilion,_New_York "Pavilion, New York"), along with [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York") and [NY 77](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_77 "New York State Route 77")—termed the "Route 63 Corridor"—were major trouble routes, primarily because of increased truck traffic using the corridor as a bypass between [I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") in Mount Morris and the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") in [Pembroke](/wiki/Pembroke,_New_York "Pembroke, New York").[[4]](#cite_note-DOT63-4) The most publicized and perhaps most fought-over possibility mentioned was that of a new expressway[[27]](#cite_note-DOTfaqs-27) from Mount Morris to Pembroke, bypassing these three routes. The [Wyoming County](/wiki/Wyoming_County,_New_York "Wyoming County, New York") Chamber of Commerce was a driving force behind this, hoping interchanges in [Perry](/wiki/Perry,_New_York "Perry, New York"), [Warsaw](/wiki/Warsaw,_New_York "Warsaw, New York") and [Attica](/wiki/Attica_(town),_New_York "Attica (town), New York") would promote business growth.[[28]](#cite_note-RPL-28)

While residents along the NY 63 corridor are against the increased truck traffic along the corridor (spurred by the [North American Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/North_American_Free_Trade_Agreement "North American Free Trade Agreement")), most of those same residents, along with other groups, also fought the proposed expressway. The general consensus of all of these groups is that NYSDOT should impose restrictions on the NY 63 corridor and force trucks to remain on I-390 and the Thruway to travel between [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") and [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania").[[28]](#cite_note-RPL-28)
